title: ECG — Acute MI Patterns
oneLiner: ECG changes indicating acute myocardial ischaemia/infarction – early recognition drives reperfusion and reduces mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them in t:
- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse/blood pressure differential, wide mediastinum on CXR. **Single finding:** BP difference >20 mmHg between arms. Do NOT give thrombolysis or full-dose anticoagulation without CT aortogram.  
- **Cardiac tamponade** – hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins. **Single finding:** electrical alternans (beat-to-beat variation in QRS amplitude) + low voltage on ECG. Requires urgent echo and pericardiocentesis – not reperfusion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Central chest pressure/crushing pain ± radiation to left arm, jaw, back or epigastrium  
  - Breathlessness, diaphoresis, nausea, light-headedness  
  - Atypical: epigastric discomfort, isolated arm/jaw pain, fatigue (especially in elderly, diabetics, women)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Pallor, clammy skin  
  - Tachycardia or bradycardia (inferior MI → bradycardia due to vagal stimulation)  
  - Hypotension (especially if RV involvement or cardiogenic shock)  
  - Signs of heart failure: elevated JVP, crackles, peripheral oedema  
  - New murmur (papillary muscle rupture, VSD) – listen carefully
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **STEMI (ST elevation)** → ST ↑ in ≥2 contiguous leads, new LBBB, or true posterior MI.  
- **NSTEMI** → ST depression/T wave inversion + troponin rise. No ST elevation.  
- **Unstable angina** → same ECG changes as NSTEMI but troponin normal.  
- **Myocarditis** → diffuse ST elevation (not anatomic distribution), often young, history of viral prodrome.  
- **Takotsubo cardiomyopathy** → apical ballooning, emotional stress trigger, ST elevation/T inversion ± mild troponin rise.  
- **Pulmonary embolism** → S1Q3T3, right ventricular strain (TWI in V1-V3), tachycardia, hypoxia.  
- **Pericarditis** → diffuse concave ST elevation, PR depression, pain worse lying flat, improves sitting forward.  
- **Aortic dissection** (see Killers First)  
- **Hyperkalaemia** → peaked T waves, sometimes ST elevation (pseudo-infarct pattern). Check K+.  
- **Early repolarisation** – benign, concave ST elevation (usually young men, no symptoms, no troponin rise).  
- **Splitter:** ST elevation that is **localised** (to coronary territory) → ischaemia; **diffuse** → pericarditis/myocarditis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival – **immediately** interpret for STEMI.  
  - If inferior STEMI: add right-sided leads (V3R-V4R) for RV MI – look for ST↑ in V4R.  
  - If high suspicion of posterior MI: add posterior leads (V7-V9) – ST↑ >0.5 mm.  
  - Repeat ECG at 15-30 min if initial non-diagnostic but symptoms persist.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - High-sensitivity troponin – 0h and 3h (or local protocol).  
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, glucose, lipid profile, coagulation screen (if considering PCI or lysis).  
  - D-dimer only if PE suspected (but troponin also needed).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR – rule out widened mediastinum (dissection), pulmonary oedema, pneumothorax.  
  - Echo – urgent if haemodynamically unstable; assess regional wall motion, RV function, mechanical complications.  
  - CT aortogram – if dissection suspected.  
- **Special tests:** None routinely.  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Universal definition of MI** – troponin rise/fall + at least one: symptoms, ECG changes, imaging evidence, or angiographic findings.  
  - **GRACE score** for NSTEMI – predicts in-hospital mortality (requires age, HR, SBP, creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest, ST deviation, elevated troponin).  
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – omission in any patient >30 with chest pain, syncope, or unexplained breathlessness is a serious error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Oxygen if saturations <94% (avoid hyperoxia if not hypoxic).  
  - Aspirin 300mg chewed stat.  
  - GTN spray 0.4mg sublingual (avoid if BP <90 mmHg or suspected RV infarct).  
  - Morphine 2.5-5mg IV for pain (if GTN insufficient) with antiemetic (metoclopramide 10mg IV).  
  - For **STEMI**:  
    - If within 12 hours of onset → arrange primary PCI (door-to-balloon <120 min).  
    - If PCI not available within 120 min → fibrinolysis (tenecteplase weight-based, per local protocol).  
    - Contraindications to fibrinolysis: previous haemorrhagic stroke, active bleeding, recent major surgery, uncontrolled hypertension >180/110.  
  - For **NSTEMI**: risk stratify with GRACE score. High-risk (GRACE >140) → early invasive strategy (angiography within 24h). Low-risk → conservative initial management.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): aspirin 75mg OD + ticagrelor 90mg BD (or prasugrel 10mg OD if PCI). [check local policy for loading dose and substituting agent]  
  - Anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin (UFH) or enoxaparin (for NSTEMI or during PCI). Doses per local protocol.  
  - Beta-blocker: bisoprolol 2.5-5mg OD (avoid if acute heart failure, bradycardia, hypotension).  
  - ACE inhibitor: ramipril 2.5mg BD (start within 24h if stable, titrate up).  
  - Statin: atorvastatin 80mg OD.  
  - Echo before discharge to assess LV function.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Lifelong aspirin 75mg OD + 12 months DAPT.  
  - ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, statin (target LDL <1.4 mmol/L).  
  - Cardiac rehabilitation.  
  - Lifestyle: smoking cessation, diet, exercise.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1L over 8h if hypotensive (but careful if heart failure); avoid fluids in RV infarct without hypotension.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD if not already anticoagulated.  
  - Diet: NBM until PCI or decision about angiography (if planned within hours).  
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, continuous ECG monitoring on coronary care unit (CCU) for first 24-48h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Aspirin: 300mg stat, then 75mg OD PO.  
  - Ticagrelor: 180mg loading, then 90mg BD PO.  
  - Heparin: UFH 60 IU/kg (max 4000 IU) bolus, then 12 IU/kg/hr (monitor APTT). Or enoxaparin 1mg/kg BD SC. [check local protocol for dosing]  
  - Fibrinolysis: tenecteplase (TNK) – weight-based (30-50mg) IV bolus. **Only for STEMI if PCI >120 min away.**  
  - Beta-blocker: metoprolol 50mg BD or bisoprolol 2.5-5mg OD.  
  - ACE inhibitor: ramipril 2.5mg BD.  
  - Statin: atorvastatin 80mg OD.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Prasugrel (60mg load, 10mg OD) – preferred in diabetics, avoid if >75yrs or low body weight.  
  - Bivalirudin – alternative to heparin during PCI.  
  - Nitrates: IV GTN (1-10mg/hr) for persistent chest pain or pulmonary oedema.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ticagrelor: avoid in history of intracranial bleed, severe hepatic impairment. Do not use with CYP3A4 strong inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole).  
  - NSAIDs – increase bleeding risk and may exacerbate heart failure. Avoid.  
  - Warfarin/DOACs – increased bleeding risk when combined with DAPT – liaise with cardiology.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (1mg/kg OD instead of BD).  
  - Ticagrelor: no adjustment for renal or mild hepatic impairment.  
  - GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors (e.g., eptifibatide) – rare, used during PCI; adjust for renal function.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Tenecteplase** – incorrect weight-based dose (underdose reduces effect; overdose increases bleeding). Use weight chart.  
  - **Heparin** – failure to monitor APTT leads to overdose bleeding or underdose re-infarction.  
  - **Insulin** – not relevant here (but note: For patients with diabetic MI, no IV insulin unless hyperglycaemia >15 mmol/L and symptomatic; give SC insulin instead – refer to local diabetes protocol).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Ventricular fibrillation/pulseless VT – immediate defibrillation.  
  - Cardiogenic shock – hypotension, cool peripheries, oliguria, raised lactate. Manage with fluids, inotropes (dobutamine), intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) if refractory.  
  - Mechanical complications: free wall rupture (sudden hypotension, electromechanical dissociation), ventricular septum rupture (new holosystolic murmur, acute right heart failure), papillary muscle rupture (new mitral regurgitation). All require urgent surgical repair.  
  - Heart block – inferior MI often causes Mobitz I or complete heart block (usually transient). Treat with atropine 500 µg IV, then transcutaneous pacing.  
  - Post-infarct pericarditis (Dressler's) – occurs days to weeks later, pericardial rub, pleuritic chest pain. Treat with colchicine or NSAIDs (avoid if on DAPT, use low-dose prednisolone).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Heart failure due to LV dysfunction.  
  - Left ventricular thrombus – echo screening; treat with warfarin (INR 2-3) for 3-6 months if an apical mural thrombus.  
  - Ventricular aneurysm – persistent ST elevation, may cause arrhythmias.  
  - Recurrent ischaemia / stent thrombosis – urgent return to catheter lab.
sections.Prognosis:
- STEMI: 30-day mortality ~5% with timely primary PCI; higher if delayed or cardiogenic shock.  
- NSTEMI: 6-month mortality 3-5% (depends on GRACE risk).  
- Left ventricular ejection fraction post-MI is the strongest predictor of long-term survival.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do right-sided ECG (V3R, V4R)** in inferior STEMI – RV infarction changes management (avoid nitrates, give fluids).  
- **Suspect posterior MI** when anterior leads (V1-V3) show horizontal ST depression with tall R waves – order posterior leads (V7-V9) to see ST elevation.  
- **Document the time to reperfusion** – target door-to-balloon ≤120 min, door-to-needle ≤30 min for lysis.
sections.Pitfalls (errors to avoid):
- **Don't give nitrates in RV infarct** – can cause profound hypotension (preload dependent). Check right-sided leads first.  
- **Don't mistake hyperkalaemic peaked T waves for ischaemia** – check K+ before activating STEMI pathway.  
- **Don't discharge a patient with suspected NSTEMI without a troponin at 3 hours** – 0h alone misses 20% of MIs.
sections.Safety netting (if patient goes home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Chest pain returns at rest, is worsening, or not relieved by GTN.  
  - New breathlessness, fainting, or palpitations.  
  - Bleeding from access site (if had angiography) – heavy or not stopping with pressure.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiac rehabilitation appointment within 2 weeks.  
  - Cardiology outpatient clinic at 4-6 weeks (or per local protocol).  
  - GP within 1 week for medication review, smoking cessation referral.
sections.Escalation criteria:
- **Call cardiac arrest team** – if loss of consciousness, no pulse, shockable rhythm (VF/pVT).  
- **Call senior (SpR/Consultant)** – for any of the following:  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially falling BP, rising HR or respiratory rate).  
  - New severe dyspnoea or pulmonary oedema.  
  - Chest pain refractory to GTN and morphine.  
  - New murmur or sign of mechanical complication.  
  - Signs of cardiogenic shock (systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >2 mmol/L, confusion, oliguria).  
  - Complete heart block (Mobitz II or third degree) – prepare for transcutaneous pacing.  
  - Recurrent ventricular arrhythmias.  
- **ICU referral** – if requires inotropes, mechanical ventilation, or continuous monitoring >48h on CCU.

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Acute MI Patterns  
**One-liner:** ECG changes indicating acute myocardial ischaemia/infarction – early recognition drives reperfusion and reduces mortality.  

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them in the First Hour?  
- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse/blood pressure differential, wide mediastinum on CXR. **Single finding:** BP difference >20 mmHg between arms. Do NOT give thrombolysis or full-dose anticoagulation without CT aortogram.  
- **Cardiac tamponade** – hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins. **Single finding:** electrical alternans (beat-to-beat variation in QRS amplitude) + low voltage on ECG. Requires urgent echo and pericardiocentesis – not reperfusion.  

### Presentation  
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Central chest pressure/crushing pain ± radiation to left arm, jaw, back or epigastrium  
  - Breathlessness, diaphoresis, nausea, light-headedness  
  - Atypical: epigastric discomfort, isolated arm/jaw pain, fatigue (especially in elderly, diabetics, women)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Pallor, clammy skin  
  - Tachycardia or bradycardia (inferior MI → bradycardia due to vagal stimulation)  
  - Hypotension (especially if RV involvement or cardiogenic shock)  
  - Signs of heart failure: elevated JVP, crackles, peripheral oedema  
  - New murmur (papillary muscle rupture, VSD) – listen carefully  

### Differential Diagnosis  
- **STEMI (ST elevation)** → ST ↑ in ≥2 contiguous leads, new LBBB, or true posterior MI.  
- **NSTEMI** → ST depression/T wave inversion + troponin rise. No ST elevation.  
- **Unstable angina** → same ECG changes as NSTEMI but troponin normal.  
- **Myocarditis** → diffuse ST elevation (not anatomic distribution), often young, history of viral prodrome.  
- **Takotsubo cardiomyopathy** → apical ballooning, emotional stress trigger, ST elevation/T inversion ± mild troponin rise.  
- **Pulmonary embolism** → S1Q3T3, right ventricular strain (TWI in V1-V3), tachycardia, hypoxia.  
- **Pericarditis** → diffuse concave ST elevation, PR depression, pain worse lying flat, improves sitting forward.  
- **Aortic dissection** (see Killers First)  
- **Hyperkalaemia** → peaked T waves, sometimes ST elevation (pseudo-infarct pattern). Check K+.  
- **Early repolarisation** – benign, concave ST elevation (usually young men, no symptoms, no troponin rise).  
- **Splitter:** ST elevation that is **localised** (to coronary territory) → ischaemia; **diffuse** → pericarditis/myocarditis.  

### Investigations  
- **Bedside:**  
  - 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival – **immediately** interpret for STEMI.  
  - If inferior STEMI: add right-sided leads (V3R-V4R) for RV MI – look for ST↑ in V4R.  
  - If high suspicion of posterior MI: add posterior leads (V7-V9) – ST↑ >0.5 mm.  
  - Repeat ECG at 15-30 min if initial non-diagnostic but symptoms persist.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - High-sensitivity troponin – 0h and 3h (or local protocol).  
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, glucose, lipid profile, coagulation screen (if considering PCI or lysis).  
  - D-dimer only if PE suspected (but troponin also needed).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR – rule out widened mediastinum (dissection), pulmonary oedema, pneumothorax.  
  - Echo – urgent if haemodynamically unstable; assess regional wall motion, RV function, mechanical complications.  
  - CT aortogram – if dissection suspected.  
- **Special tests:** None routinely.  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Universal definition of MI** – troponin rise/fall + at least one: symptoms, ECG changes, imaging evidence, or angiographic findings.  
  - **GRACE score** for NSTEMI – predicts in-hospital mortality (requires age, HR, SBP, creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest, ST deviation, elevated troponin).  
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – omission in any patient >30 with chest pain, syncope, or unexplained breathlessness is a serious error.  

### Management  
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Oxygen if saturations <94% (avoid hyperoxia if not hypoxic).  
  - Aspirin 300mg chewed stat.  
  - GTN spray 0.4mg sublingual (avoid if BP <90 mmHg or suspected RV infarct).  
  - Morphine 2.5-5mg IV for pain (if GTN insufficient) with antiemetic (metoclopramide 10mg IV).  
  - For **STEMI**:  
    - If within 12 hours of onset → arrange primary PCI (door-to-balloon <120 min).  
    - If PCI not available within 120 min → fibrinolysis (tenecteplase weight-based, per local protocol).  
    - Contraindications to fibrinolysis: previous haemorrhagic stroke, active bleeding, recent major surgery, uncontrolled hypertension >180/110.  
  - For **NSTEMI**: risk stratify with GRACE score. High-risk (GRACE >140) → early invasive strategy (angiography within 24h). Low-risk → conservative initial management.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): aspirin 75mg OD + ticagrelor 90mg BD (or prasugrel 10mg OD if PCI). [check local policy for loading dose and substituting agent]  
  - Anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin (UFH) or enoxaparin (for NSTEMI or during PCI). Doses per local protocol.  
  - Beta-blocker: bisoprolol 2.5-5mg OD (avoid if acute heart failure, bradycardia, hypotension).  
  - ACE inhibitor: ramipril 2.5mg BD (start within 24h if stable, titrate up).  
  - Statin: atorvastatin 80mg OD.  
  - Echo before discharge to assess LV function.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Lifelong aspirin 75mg OD + 12 months DAPT.  
  - ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, statin (target LDL <1.4 mmol/L).  
  - Cardiac rehabilitation.  
  - Lifestyle: smoking cessation, diet, exercise.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1L over 8h if hypotensive (but careful if heart failure); avoid fluids in RV infarct without hypotension.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD if not already anticoagulated.  
  - Diet: NBM until PCI or decision about angiography (if planned within hours).  
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, continuous ECG monitoring on coronary care unit (CCU) for first 24-48h.  

### Drugs & Doses  
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Aspirin: 300mg stat, then 75mg OD PO.  
  - Ticagrelor: 180mg loading, then 90mg BD PO.  
  - Heparin: UFH 60 IU/kg (max 4000 IU) bolus, then 12 IU/kg/hr (monitor APTT). Or enoxaparin 1mg/kg BD SC. [check local protocol for dosing]  
  - Fibrinolysis: tenecteplase (TNK) – weight-based (30-50mg) IV bolus. **Only for STEMI if PCI >120 min away.**  
  - Beta-blocker: metoprolol 50mg BD or bisoprolol 2.5-5mg OD.  
  - ACE inhibitor: ramipril 2.5mg BD.  
  - Statin: atorvastatin 80mg OD.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Prasugrel (60mg load, 10mg OD) – preferred in diabetics, avoid if >75yrs or low body weight.  
  - Bivalirudin – alternative to heparin during PCI.  
  - Nitrates: IV GTN (1-10mg/hr) for persistent chest pain or pulmonary oedema.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ticagrelor: avoid in history of intracranial bleed, severe hepatic impairment. Do not use with CYP3A4 strong inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole).  
  - NSAIDs – increase bleeding risk and may exacerbate heart failure. Avoid.  
  - Warfarin/DOACs – increased bleeding risk when combined with DAPT – liaise with cardiology.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (1mg/kg OD instead of BD).  
  - Ticagrelor: no adjustment for renal or mild hepatic impairment.  
  - GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors (e.g., eptifibatide) – rare, used during PCI; adjust for renal function.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Tenecteplase** – incorrect weight-based dose (underdose reduces effect; overdose increases bleeding). Use weight chart.  
  - **Heparin** – failure to monitor APTT leads to overdose bleeding or underdose re-infarction.  
  - **Insulin** – not relevant here (but note: For patients with diabetic MI, no IV insulin unless hyperglycaemia >15 mmol/L and symptomatic; give SC insulin instead – refer to local diabetes protocol).  

### Complications  
- **Acute:**  
  - Ventricular fibrillation/pulseless VT – immediate defibrillation.  
  - Cardiogenic shock – hypotension, cool peripheries, oliguria, raised lactate. Manage with fluids, inotropes (dobutamine), intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) if refractory.  
  - Mechanical complications: free wall rupture (sudden hypotension, electromechanical dissociation), ventricular septum rupture (new holosystolic murmur, acute right heart failure), papillary muscle rupture (new mitral regurgitation). All require urgent surgical repair.  
  - Heart block – inferior MI often causes Mobitz I or complete heart block (usually transient). Treat with atropine 500 µg IV, then transcutaneous pacing.  
  - Post-infarct pericarditis (Dressler's) – occurs days to weeks later, pericardial rub, pleuritic chest pain. Treat with colchicine or NSAIDs (avoid if on DAPT, use low-dose prednisolone).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Heart failure due to LV dysfunction.  
  - Left ventricular thrombus – echo screening; treat with warfarin (INR 2-3) for 3-6 months if an apical mural thrombus.  
  - Ventricular aneurysm – persistent ST elevation, may cause arrhythmias.  
  - Recurrent ischaemia / stent thrombosis – urgent return to catheter lab.  

### Prognosis  
- STEMI: 30-day mortality ~5% with timely primary PCI; higher if delayed or cardiogenic shock.  
- NSTEMI: 6-month mortality 3-5% (depends on GRACE risk).  
- Left ventricular ejection fraction post-MI is the strongest predictor of long-term survival.  

### Pearls (positive actions)  
- **Always do right-sided ECG (V3R, V4R)** in inferior STEMI – RV infarction changes management (avoid nitrates, give fluids).  
- **Suspect posterior MI** when anterior leads (V1-V3) show horizontal ST depression with tall R waves – order posterior leads (V7-V9) to see ST elevation.  
- **Document the time to reperfusion** – target door-to-balloon ≤120 min, door-to-needle ≤30 min for lysis.  

### Pitfalls (errors to avoid)  
- **Don't give nitrates in RV infarct** – can cause profound hypotension (preload dependent). Check right-sided leads first.  
- **Don't mistake hyperkalaemic peaked T waves for ischaemia** – check K+ before activating STEMI pathway.  
- **Don't discharge a patient with suspected NSTEMI without a troponin at 3 hours** – 0h alone misses 20% of MIs.  

### Safety netting (if patient goes home)  
- **Return if:**  
  - Chest pain returns at rest, is worsening, or not relieved by GTN.  
  - New breathlessness, fainting, or palpitations.  
  - Bleeding from access site (if had angiography) – heavy or not stopping with pressure.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiac rehabilitation appointment within 2 weeks.  
  - Cardiology outpatient clinic at 4-6 weeks (or per local protocol).  
  - GP within 1 week for medication review, smoking cessation referral.  

### Escalation criteria  
- **Call cardiac arrest team** – if loss of consciousness, no pulse, shockable rhythm (VF/pVT).  
- **Call senior (SpR/Consultant)** – for any of the following:  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially falling BP, rising HR or respiratory rate).  
  - New severe dyspnoea or pulmonary oedema.  
  - Chest pain refractory to GTN and morphine.  
  - New murmur or sign of mechanical complication.  
  - Signs of cardiogenic shock (systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >2 mmol/L, confusion, oliguria).  
  - Complete heart block (Mobitz II or third degree) – prepare for transcutaneous pacing.  
  - Recurrent ventricular arrhythmias.  
- **ICU referral** – if requires inotropes, mechanical ventilation, or continuous monitoring >48h on CCU.
